topic: Müller, K., & Zhang, L. (2023). Quantifying confabulation in autoregressive language models: The confabulation coefficient and cascade effects. Journal of Artificial Intelligence Research, 78(4), 1123–1145. doi:10.48550/arXiv.2305.12345
final_review:
The concept of confabulation in autoregressive language models has gained significant attention in recent years, with researchers exploring its implications and potential applications. One of the key challenges in this area is detecting hallucinations in large language models, which can be achieved using semantic entropy (Farquhar et al., 2024). The use of large language models in healthcare is another area of research, with studies exploring their potential applications in medical imaging (Bradshaw et al., 2025) and cardiovascular magnetic resonance reports (Salam et al., 2024). However, the evaluation of large language models in healthcare requires a framework that takes into account human factors.

The concept of confabulation is not limited to language models, and researchers have explored its implications in cognitive science and neuroscience. Furthermore, researchers have explored the potential applications of large language models in education (Polverini and Gregorcic, 2023) and creative problem-solving (Boussioux et al., 2024). These studies demonstrate the versatility of large language models and their potential to support human cognition. However, the development of "ecologically valid" measures of executive function is essential for evaluating the effectiveness of large language models in real-world settings (Burgess et al., 2006).

In addition to these studies, researchers have investigated the effects of large language models on various aspects of human cognition. The use of large language models in different domains highlights the need for a comprehensive understanding of their capabilities and limitations. As research in this area continues to evolve, it is essential to consider the implications of large language models for human cognition and behavior.

In conclusion, the study of confabulation in autoregressive language models is a complex and multifaceted area of research, with implications for cognitive science, neuroscience, and healthcare. Further research is needed to fully understand the mechanisms underlying confabulation and to develop effective strategies for mitigating its effects.

References:
Boussioux, L., Lane, J. N., & Zhang, M. (2024). The Crowdless Future? Generative AI and Creative Problem-Solving. https://doi.org/10.1287/orsc.2023.18430
Bradshaw, T., Tie, X., & Warner, J. (2025). Large Language Models and Large Multimodal Models in Medical Imaging: A Primer for Physicians. https://doi.org/10.2967/jnumed.124.268072
Burgess, P. W., Alderman, N., & Forbes, C. (2006). The case for the development and use of “ecologically valid” measures of executive function in experimental and clinical neuropsychology. https://doi.org/10.1017/s1355617706060310
Farquhar, S., Kossen, J., & Kuhn, L. (2024). Detecting hallucinations in large language models using semantic entropy. https://doi.org/10.1038/s41586-024-07421-0
Polverini, G., & Gregorcic, B. (2023). How understanding large language models can inform the use of ChatGPT in physics education. https://doi.org/10.1088/1361-6404/ad1420
Salam, B., Kravchenko, D., & Nowak, S. (2024). Generative Pre-trained Transformer 4 makes cardiovascular magnetic resonance reports easy to understand. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jocmr.2024.101035
